calculateurs.title: Calculators
calculateurs.description: All the calculation tools for your training

hub.seoDescription: Running calculators: training zones, race paces, treadmill converter, split generator, VMA estimation.

zones.seoDescription: Calculate your 6 heart rate and pace training zones from VMA or max HR. Free tool to personalize your running zones with FC ranges and pace targets.
zones.seoAppDescription: Calculate your 6 heart rate and pace training zones from your VMA or max HR
zones.title: Training Zones Calculator
zones.description: Enter your max heart rate (FCmax) or VMA to calculate your 6 personalized training zones with heart rate ranges and pace targets.

paces.seoDescription: Race pace calculator: estimate your finish times from 5K to marathon based on VMA. Convert speed to pace with predicted race times for every distance.
paces.seoAppDescription: Estimate your race times from 5K to marathon based on your VMA
paces.title: Race Pace Calculator
paces.description: Enter your VMA to estimate your race paces and predicted finish times for distances from 5K to marathon.

converter.seoDescription: Free pace converter for runners. Convert between min/km, km/h and min/mile in real time with automatic training zone detection based on your VMA.
converter.seoAppDescription: Convert between min/km, km/h and min/mile in real time
converter.title: Pace Converter
converter.description: Type in any field and the others update instantly.
converter.displayingUnits: Displaying in {{system}} units. Change in Settings.

vma.seoDescription: Calculate your VMA (Maximal Aerobic Speed) free from a race result. Enter your 5K, 10K, half marathon or marathon time to estimate VMA and pace zones.
vma.seoAppDescription: Estimate your VMA from a recent race result
vma.title: VMA from Race Time
vma.description: Enter a recent race time to estimate your VMA (Maximal Aerobic Speed) and see your training zones.

splits.seoDescription: Split generator for runners: plan per-km race splits for your target time. Even, negative, or positive split strategies from 5K to marathon distances.
splits.seoAppDescription: Generate per-km splits for your target race time with even, negative, or positive split strategies
splits.title: Split Generator
splits.description: Plan your race splits to reach your target time.
splits.evenDesc: All splits at the same pace.
splits.emptyState: Enter a distance and target time to generate your splits.

equivalence.seoDescription: Race time equivalence calculator using the Riegel formula. Predict your finish times across 5K, 10K, half marathon, and marathon from one race result.
equivalence.seoAppDescription: Predict your race times across all distances from a recent result
equivalence.title: Race Equivalence
equivalence.description: Enter a race result to predict your times across all standard distances.
equivalence.riegelExplanation: Based on Riegel's formula (1977), used by most running calculators. More reliable between similar distances (5K→10K) than distant ones (5K→Marathon).

ageGraded.seoDescription: Age-graded performance calculator for runners. Compare your race time to the age and gender adjusted world record and discover your performance level.
ageGraded.seoAppDescription: Compare your performance to the age and gender adjusted world record
ageGraded.title: Age-Graded Performance
ageGraded.description: Enter your age, gender, distance and time to see how your performance compares to the world record for your category.
ageGraded.explanation: The age-graded score compares your performance to the world record adjusted for your age and gender. A score of 70% means you run at 70% of the world record level for your category.

paceTable.seoDescription: Complete pace reference table from 3:00 to 10:00/km with speed zones and estimated race times for 5K, 10K, half-marathon and marathon distances.
paceTable.seoAppDescription: Complete pace reference table from 3:00 to 10:00/km with estimated race times
paceTable.title: Pace Reference Table
paceTable.subtitle: All paces from 3:00 to 10:00/km with estimated race times.
paceTable.highlightNote: The highlighted row is closest to your 100% VMA pace.

treadmill.seoDescription: Treadmill pace converter: calculate equivalent outdoor pace from treadmill speed and incline. Includes training zone detection for your running effort.
treadmill.seoAppDescription: Convert treadmill speed and incline to equivalent outdoor pace
treadmill.title: Treadmill Converter
treadmill.description: Convert treadmill speed and incline to equivalent outdoor pace.
treadmill.configureVma: Configure your VMA in My Zones to see the training zone.
treadmill.formula: The formula accounts for the lack of air resistance and incline effect. A 1% incline roughly compensates for indoor running. Each additional 1% incline adds approximately 3% to the effort.

whatIf.seoDescription: Compare two training scenarios side by side. Visualize the impact of training frequency, goal, and duration on your plan.
whatIf.seoAppDescription: Compare two training scenarios side by side
whatIf.title: What-If Simulator
whatIf.saveDescription: Give your scenario comparison a name to find it later.
whatIf.deleteDescription: This scenario will be permanently deleted. This action cannot be undone.

methodology.title: Methodology
methodology.seoDescription: The scientific foundations behind Zoned's 6-zone training system. Based on established sports science research.
methodology.heading: The Science behind Zoned
methodology.intro: Every workout in Zoned is grounded in decades of sports science research. Here are the physiological principles and key studies that shape our training zones.
methodology.ourApproachText1: All workouts in Zoned are based on established sports science. The intensity zones are not arbitrary: they correspond to real physiological thresholds that can be measured in a lab or estimated from field tests.
methodology.ourApproachText2: Our 6-zone model is derived from key physiological markers: the first ventilatory threshold (VT1), the second ventilatory threshold (VT2, also called the lactate turn point), and maximal oxygen uptake (VO2max). These thresholds define distinct metabolic states that require specific training stimuli.
methodology.ourApproachDisclaimer: Important: The percentage-based zones presented here are approximations. Heart rate at LT1 and LT2 varies significantly between individuals. Lab testing provides the most accurate boundaries for your personal zones.

planMethodology.title: Plan Guide
planMethodology.seoDescription: The 7 evidence-based principles behind Zoned's training plan generation. Understand how your plan is built.
planMethodology.heading: How Zoned Builds Your Plan
planMethodology.intro: Every training plan in Zoned is generated from 7 evidence-based principles. No black box: here is exactly how your plan is built, and why.
planMethodology.introText: The plan generator combines periodization models from Daniels and Lydiard, the polarized training distribution validated by Seiler, volume progression rules derived from Gabbett's acute:chronic workload research, and the exponential taper model of Mujika & Padilla. Each parameter is adapted to your race distance, fitness level, and training goal.
planMethodology.introDisclaimer: These are the same principles used by professional coaches. The difference is that Zoned applies them automatically based on your profile.

about.seoDescription: Learn about Zoned, an open-source running workout library with zone-based training.
about.seoOrgDescription: An open-source running workout library with zone-based training.

home.seoDescription: {{count}} science-based running workouts organized by training zones. Free workout library for runners of all levels.

articleFaq.q1: My watch shows Zone 4 but I feel like I'm in Zone 2. Who should I trust?
articleFaq.a1: Trust your sensations. Heart rate can be skewed by heat, stress, fatigue, caffeine, and sensor position. The golden rule for Zone 2: if you can hold a normal conversation without effort, you're in the right zone regardless of what your watch displays.
articleFaq.q2: Should I stay exactly in my zone?
articleFaq.a2: No, zones are indicative ranges, not beat-precise targets. Being 3-5 bpm above or below the limit has no significant physiological importance. What matters is respecting the session's objective.
articleFaq.q3: How many times per week should I run?
articleFaq.a3: Beginners (< 1 year): 2-3 outings per week. Regular runners: 3-5 outings covering most goals from 10K to marathon, alternating hard and easy days. Advanced runners: 5-7 outings, sometimes doubles, requiring years of gradual progression.
articleFaq.q4: Is the 10% rule a myth or reality?
articleFaq.a4: The 10% rule (don't increase weekly volume by more than 10%) is a useful guardrail, not an absolute physiological law. It forces progressivity, but doesn't account for training history. Use it as a guiding principle, not a rigid rule.
articleFaq.a5: First cardiovascular changes appear as early as 2-3 weeks of regular training. Measurable progress on a test or race generally arrives after 6-12 weeks of consistent work. Patience is crucial - changing programs every 3 weeks never lets adaptations settle.
articleFaq.q6: I'm in pain during the run. Continue or stop?
articleFaq.a6: Distinguish discomfort from pain. Discomfort (heaviness, slight stiffness, soreness) is often normal and usually passes after 10-15 minutes. Pain (sharp, localized, increasing or causing limping): stop immediately. When in doubt, a session missed out of caution is always preferable to weeks of forced rest.
articleFaq.q7: How do I prevent running injuries?
articleFaq.a7: Progress gradually (most injuries come from increasing volume or intensity too quickly). Vary stimuli (different terrains, shoes in rotation, different paces). Do strength training 15-20 minutes twice a week for hamstrings, quads, calves and glutes. Listen to warning signals early.
articleFaq.q8: Morning or evening, what's better for running?
articleFaq.a8: Physiologically, evening has a slight advantage: higher body temperature, more mobile joints, better reaction time. But the best time is when you actually run. Consistency beats theoretical optimization.
articleFaq.a9: It's possible but with precautions: alternate easy and hard days, keep at least 1-2 days per week very light or cross-training (cycling, swimming), and listen to your body. Many runners thrive with 5-6 days per week and 1-2 days of complete rest. Rest is part of training.
